key_insight: Plastic deformation can be modeled without assuming a crystal lattice: the paper proposes an oblate-spheroid transformation zone (OSTZ) as the fundamental carrier, with dislocations emerging only as collective, large-scale excitations.
engineering_relevance: This could extend plasticity modeling to grain boundaries, glasses, ceramics, and nanocrystals where classical dislocation theory is hard to apply.
method: Use Eshelby inclusion theory to represent a shear-eigenstrained oblate spheroidal transformation zone (OSTZ).
Show that a single OSTZ has a finite, non-singular elastic dipole field.
Derive how a co-planar chain of OSTZs becomes mathematically equivalent to a Peierls-Nabarro dislocation, with a dislocation nucleating only after a critical chain length.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation against grain-boundary-sliding data and literature across metals, ceramics, and bulk metallic glasses, plus a 41-system compilation with eigenstrain fits within 2% (dilatational) and 15% (shear).
watch_item: The framework is theory-driven and depends on fitting OSTZ eigenstrains, so its practical predictive power will hinge on how robustly those parameters transfer across materials and loading paths.
paper_title: Oblate Spheroid Excitation Theory: A Unified, Lattice-Free Foundation for Plastic Deformation from Which Dislocations Emerge as Collective Excitations
